planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in early spring; lady’s mantle can also be direct sown on the soil surface in early spring as the seed needs light and moisture to germinate. It self-sows freely once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade; lady’s mantle is treasured for its large, rounded, softly pleated and densely hairy leaves that bead morning dew and rain into perfect silver droplets — one of the most beloved effects in the garden. The frothy sprays of tiny chartreuse-yellow flowers bloom over many weeks in early summer, creating an airy effect that blends beautifully with everything. Shear spent flower stems and old foliage back hard in midsummer to produce a flush of fresh new growth. In warm climates (zone 7 and up) lady’s mantle may go dormant in summer heat — it strongly prefers cool, moist conditions and performs best in the Pacific Northwest and Upper South in partial shade.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring on moist soil, or divide established clumps in early spring or fall; great burnet transplants easily from container-grown stock and establishes quickly in moist sites.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; great burnet is a stately perennial producing airy, branched stems topped with small oval heads of deep red to maroon flowers from midsummer through fall. The fine-cut blue-green compound leaves have the same cool, cucumber-like flavor as salad burnet (Sanguisorba minor) and can be used fresh in salads and drinks. Great burnet has become fashionable in contemporary naturalistic planting designs, where its tall airy flower stems weave beautifully through grasses and other meadow perennials. Japanese cultivars (S. obtusa, S. tenuifolia) with longer, more elegant flower spikes are widely used in hybrid plantings. Tolerates moist to occasionally wet soil.

planting.note: Plant bare-root divisions in early spring or set out container plants; snowdrop anemone spreads by stolons and will form expanding colonies over time. Fall division is also possible.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade; snowdrop anemone is a delightful spring-blooming European native producing pure white 2-inch flowers with prominent yellow stamens above deeply cut, soft green leaves. It blooms for 4–6 weeks in mid-spring and often produces a second flush of bloom in fall, making it exceptional value among shade perennials. After blooming, fluffy white seedheads resembling small puffballs follow the flowers. The plant spreads steadily by underground stolons to form a colony but is not aggressively invasive — simply pull unwanted shoots to contain the spread. It performs best in climates with cool springs and is an outstanding companion for spring bulbs under deciduous trees.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted stolons in early spring in moist, shaded sites; Meehan’s mint roots readily along its running stems and new plantlets can be separated and transplanted during the growing season.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Meehan’s mint is a native North American groundcover in the mint family related to the Asian Meehania species, producing small heart-shaped leaves on creeping stems that root at the nodes as they spread. In spring it produces beautiful tubular purple-blue flowers similar to a scaled-down version of ajuga or lamium. The entire plant is mildly mint-scented. It is genuinely shade-tolerant and performs in the deep, dry shade under large oaks and maples where many groundcovers struggle. It spreads at a moderate pace and is easy to control, making it a refined rather than invasive groundcover choice for woodland gardens. An excellent native companion for jack-in-the-pulpit, ferns, and wild ginger.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring or fall in shaded, moist to moderately dry sites; greater woodrush establishes slowly but once settled it is essentially permanent and requires no maintenance.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; greater woodrush is a broad-leaved, rush-like European woodlander that forms handsome, slowly expanding evergreen clumps of rich green foliage throughout the year. Unlike lawn grasses, it thrives in the deep dry shade under dense tree canopies. In spring it produces airy, branched clusters of tiny rusty-brown flowers on arching stems. The golden-leaved cultivar ‘Aurea’ brightens dark corners with vivid chartreuse-yellow winter foliage that fades to green in summer. It is one of the most effective and lowest-maintenance shade groundcovers for difficult dry shade, and the evergreen foliage provides year-round ground cover where deciduous plants leave a seasonal gap.
